title: Microbiology & Infectious Disease — USMLE Step 1
description: Gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria, viruses, fungi, parasites, antibiotics, and key clinical infectious disease scenarios.
subject: medicine

cards.front: Gram-positive cocci in clusters: organism and key infections
cards.back: Staphylococcus aureus. Infections: skin (furuncles, impetigo), pneumonia (post-influenza), endocarditis (IV drug users), osteomyelitis, toxic shock syndrome, food poisoning (preformed toxin)

cards.front: Streptococcus pyogenes (Group A Strep): infections and complications
cards.back: Pharyngitis, impetigo, scarlet fever, cellulitis, necrotizing fasciitis. Complications: rheumatic fever (pharyngitis), post-streptococcal GN (pharyngitis or skin infection)

cards.front: Streptococcus pneumoniae: capsule and clinical significance
cards.back: Polysaccharide capsule (antibody-mediated opsonization). Most common cause: pneumonia, otitis media, meningitis (adults), sinusitis. Quellung reaction: capsule swells with specific antibody

cards.front: Lancefield grouping: which group for Strep pyogenes vs Strep agalactiae?
cards.back: Group A: S. pyogenes (pharyngitis, RF). Group B: S. agalactiae (neonatal meningitis and sepsis, GBS prophylaxis in pregnancy)

cards.front: MRSA: treatment
cards.back: Vancomycin (IV for serious infections). Alternatives: linezolid, daptomycin, ceftaroline, TMP-SMX (for skin/soft tissue community-acquired MRSA)

cards.front: Staphylococcus epidermidis: clinical significance
cards.back: Normal skin flora; pathogen in prosthetic valve endocarditis, catheter-related bloodstream infections, prosthetic joint infections. Produces biofilm. CONS (coagulase-negative Staph)

cards.front: Clostridium difficile: treatment of mild/moderate vs severe disease
cards.back: Mild/moderate: oral vancomycin or fidaxomicin (metronidazole now second-line). Severe: oral vancomycin ± IV metronidazole. Recurrent: fecal microbiota transplant (FMT)

cards.front: E. coli: virulence factors by pathogenic strain
cards.back: ETEC: heat-labile (cAMP) and heat-stable (cGMP) toxins → traveler's diarrhea. EHEC O157:H7: Shiga-like toxin → bloody diarrhea, HUS. EAEC: biofilm. EPEC: A/E lesions (pediatric diarrhea)

cards.front: Salmonella vs Shigella: key differences
cards.back: Salmonella: can persist in RES (osteomyelitis in sickle cell), carrier state in gallbladder (typhoid). Shigella: more infectious (10 organisms), bloody diarrhea, dysentery. Both: fecal-oral

cards.front: Listeria monocytogenes: infections and treatment
cards.back: Neonatal meningitis (most common in neonates after GBS), meningitis in immunocompromised/elderly, intrauterine infection. Deli meats, soft cheeses, raw produce. Treatment: ampicillin

cards.front: Pseudomonas aeruginosa: clinical settings and treatment
cards.back: Burns, CF, nosocomial pneumonia, UTI in catheterized, IV drug users (osteomyelitis). Produces blue-green pigment (pyocyanin). Treatment: anti-pseudomonal β-lactam + aminoglycoside or fluoroquinolone

cards.front: Neisseria gonorrhoeae: treatment (2024 guidelines)
cards.back: Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose (increase to 1g if weight ≥ 150 kg). If chlamydia not excluded: add doxycycline

cards.front: Neisseria meningitidis: features and prophylaxis
cards.back: Petechial/purpuric rash, meningococcemia, Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome (bilateral adrenal hemorrhage). Prophylaxis for close contacts: rifampin or ciprofloxacin or ceftriaxone IM

cards.front: Helicobacter pylori: virulence factor and clinical significance
cards.back: Urease (creates alkaline environment), VacA, CagA. Causes peptic ulcer disease, gastric adenocarcinoma, MALT lymphoma

cards.front: Bordetella pertussis: toxin and stages of whooping cough
cards.back: Pertussis toxin (ADP-ribosylates Gi → ↑ cAMP). Stages: Catarrhal (cold-like) → Paroxysmal (whooping cough + post-tussive vomiting) → Convalescent. Treatment: azithromycin

cards.front: Tuberculosis: key virulence factor and type of immunity
cards.back: Cord factor (trehalose dimycolate) → induces granuloma. Cell-mediated immunity (type IV hypersensitivity). PPD/TST detects prior exposure (positive = ≥ 5, 10, or 15 mm depending on risk)

cards.front: Syphilis stages: primary, secondary, tertiary findings
cards.back: Primary: painless chancre. Secondary: diffuse maculopapular rash (palms + soles), condyloma lata, fever, lymphadenopathy. Tertiary: gummas, aortic root aneurysm, tabes dorsalis, Argyll Robertson pupils

cards.front: Treponema pallidum: diagnostic tests
cards.back: Non-treponemal (screening): VDRL, RPR. Treponemal (confirmatory): FTA-ABS, TPPA. VDRL false positives: VDRL (+) in SLE, mononucleosis, pregnancy, leprosy, malaria (SLE Makes People Lose Minds)

cards.front: Lyme disease: stages and organism
cards.back: Borrelia burgdorferi (Ixodes tick). Stage 1: erythema migrans (bull's-eye rash). Stage 2: neurological (Bell's palsy, meningitis), cardiac (heart block). Stage 3: migratory arthritis. Treatment: doxycycline

cards.front: HIV: viral proteins and tests
cards.back: gp120 (binds CD4), gp41 (membrane fusion), p24 (capsid antigen — earliest marker). Tests: HIV RNA (viral load, earliest), p24 antigen + anti-HIV antibodies (4th gen combo test), Western blot (confirmatory)

cards.front: CD4 count and opportunistic infections
cards.back: < 500: candida, HSV, VZV, hairy leukoplakia. < 200: PCP, toxoplasmosis, cryptococcal meningitis. < 50: CMV retinitis, MAC, CNS lymphoma (EBV-driven)

cards.front: Candida albicans: germ tube test and disease states
cards.back: Germ tube test positive at 37°C (differentiates from other Candida). Infections: thrush (oral/vaginal), esophagitis (worst in AIDS with CD4 < 100), systemic candidiasis. Treatment: fluconazole (topical/systemic)

cards.front: Cryptococcus neoformans: encapsulated yeast and treatment
cards.back: Polysaccharide capsule (India ink prep shows halo). Meningitis in AIDS (CD4 < 100). Latex agglutination for antigen. Treatment: amphotericin B + flucytosine (induction) → fluconazole (maintenance)

cards.front: Aspergillus fumigatus: clinical presentations
cards.back: Invasive aspergillosis (immunocompromised), aspergilloma (fungal ball in pre-existing cavity), allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA in asthma/CF). Treatment: voriconazole

cards.front: Histoplasma capsulatum: exposure and findings
cards.back: Ohio/Mississippi River Valley, bat/bird droppings. Intracellular yeast in macrophages. Mimics TB (apical lung lesions). Disseminated in AIDS. Dx: urine antigen, BAL, serology. Treatment: itraconazole (mild) or amphotericin (severe)

cards.front: Coccidioides immitis: exposure and finding
cards.back: Southwest US (California, Arizona), construction workers. Spherules with endospores on biopsy (NOT budding yeast). San Joaquin Valley fever. Treatment: fluconazole or itraconazole

cards.front: Mechanisms of antibiotic resistance
cards.back: β-lactamase (destroys β-lactam ring), altered binding site (MRSA: altered PBP2a), efflux pumps (tetracycline, fluoroquinolones), altered outer membrane (Gram-negatives), enzymatic inactivation (aminoglycosides)

cards.front: β-lactam antibiotics: mechanism
cards.back: Inhibit cell wall synthesis by binding to PBPs (penicillin-binding proteins) → prevent transpeptidation of peptidoglycan. Bactericidal. Ineffective against organisms without cell wall (Mycoplasma)

cards.front: Aminoglycosides: mechanism, toxicity, monitoring
cards.back: Bind 30S ribosome → misreading of mRNA. Bactericidal. Toxicity: nephrotoxicity (proximal tubule), ototoxicity (irreversible vestibular/cochlear). Monitor peak (efficacy) and trough (toxicity)

cards.front: Fluoroquinolones: mechanism and contraindications
cards.back: Inhibit DNA gyrase (Gram-negatives) and topoisomerase IV (Gram-positives) → DNA strand breaks. Bactericidal. Avoid: children (cartilage toxicity), pregnancy, myasthenia gravis (↑ weakness). QT prolongation

cards.front: Tetracyclines: mechanism and contraindications
cards.back: Bind 30S ribosome → inhibit tRNA attachment. Bacteriostatic. Contraindicated: children < 8 (dental staining, bone growth inhibition), pregnancy. Doxycycline: treats atypicals, Lyme, RMSF, chlamydia

cards.front: Vancomycin: mechanism, uses, toxicity
cards.back: Inhibits cell wall synthesis by binding D-Ala-D-Ala terminal residues of peptidoglycan. Active against Gram-positives only. Uses: MRSA, C. diff (oral). Toxicity: red man syndrome (infusion-related, not allergy), nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity

cards.front: Macrolides (azithromycin, clarithromycin, erythromycin): mechanism
cards.back: Bind 50S ribosome → inhibit translocation (peptidyl transferase). Bacteriostatic. Uses: atypical pneumonia (Mycoplasma, Chlamydia, Legionella), STIs, H. pylori, MAC prophylaxis

cards.front: Linezolid: mechanism and unique adverse effect
cards.back: Binds 50S ribosome → inhibits 70S ribosomal initiation complex. Bacteriostatic for Gram-positives. Unique: reversible MAO inhibition → serotonin syndrome risk with SSRIs/MAOIs

cards.front: Herpes simplex virus (HSV): types and treatment
cards.back: HSV-1: oral herpes/cold sores, herpes encephalitis (temporal lobe). HSV-2: genital herpes. Treatment: acyclovir (nucleoside analogue; needs viral thymidine kinase for phosphorylation)

cards.front: CMV: clinical scenarios by immune status
cards.back: Immunocompetent: mononucleosis-like (heterophile-negative). Immunocompromised: retinitis (AIDS, CD4 < 50), colitis, esophagitis, pneumonia, encephalitis. Treatment: ganciclovir (prophylaxis: valganciclovir)

cards.front: EBV (infectious mononucleosis): hetero­phile antibodies and complications
cards.back: Monospot test (heterophile antibody test) positive. Causes lymphoma (Burkitt), nasopharyngeal carcinoma, PTLD. Complications: splenic rupture, rash with amoxicillin

cards.front: Influenza: neuraminidase inhibitors mechanism and timing
cards.back: Oseltamivir (Tamiflu) and zanamivir block neuraminidase → virus cannot bud from host cell. Most effective within 48 hours of symptom onset

cards.front: Rabies: transmission and vaccine strategy
cards.back: Animal bite (bats, raccoons, foxes, dogs). Virus travels retrograde via peripheral nerves. PEP: wound care + RIG (rabies immune globulin at wound site) + vaccine series (days 0, 3, 7, 14)

cards.front: Hepatitis B treatment indications and drugs
cards.back: Treat if HBV DNA > 2000 IU/mL with liver disease, or cirrhosis regardless. Drugs: tenofovir (TDF or TAF) or entecavir (preferred for long-term treatment)

cards.front: Rickettsia rickettsii (RMSF): clinical features and treatment
cards.back: Transmitted by Dermacentor tick. Fever + headache + rash (starts on wrists/ankles, spreads centrally, includes palms/soles). Treatment: doxycycline (empirical, do NOT wait for serology)

cards.front: Malaria: Plasmodium species and chloroquine resistance
cards.back: P. vivax and P. ovale: relapsing (dormant hypnozoites in liver — treat with primaquine). P. falciparum: most severe, cerebral malaria, chloroquine-resistant worldwide. P. malariae: quartan fever (48hr cycle). Treatment of resistant: artemisinin-based combination

cards.front: Toxoplasma gondii: transmission and risk groups
cards.back: Definitive host: cats (cysts in feces). Intermediate: humans/animals (eat undercooked meat). Congenital: TORCH (chorioretinitis, hydrocephalus, intracranial calcifications). AIDS (CD4 < 100): encephalitis (ring-enhancing lesions). Treatment: pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine

cards.front: Empiric antibiotic for community-acquired pneumonia (outpatient, no comorbidities)
cards.back: Amoxicillin + azithromycin OR respiratory fluoroquinolone (levofloxacin/moxifloxacin) monotherapy

cards.front: Fungal infections: Candida — risk factors and treatment
cards.back: Risk factors: immunosuppression, broad-spectrum antibiotics (↓ normal flora), indwelling catheters, TPN, diabetes. Candida albicans: germ tubes at 37°C (pseudohyphae). Oral thrush: fluconazole. Esophageal: fluconazole (AIDS-defining illness). Invasive/candidemia: echinocandin (anidulafungin/micafungin) — preferred in critically ill; amphotericin B for CNS/ocular.

cards.front: Tuberculosis: primary vs secondary, classic radiographic findings
cards.back: Primary TB: lower/middle lobe consolidation, Ghon focus (subpleural calcification) + hilar lymphadenopathy = Ranke complex. Latent: positive TST/IGRA, asymptomatic. Secondary (reactivation): upper lobe cavitary lesion, apical fibrosis, miliary pattern (hematogenous). RIPE therapy: Rifampin, Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol ×2 months, then RI ×4 months.

cards.front: Staphylococcus aureus: virulence factors and toxin-mediated syndromes
cards.back: Protein A: binds IgG Fc (immune evasion). Coagulase: fibrin clot. Toxins: TSST-1 (toxic shock — superantigen, massive cytokine release), exfoliatin (scalded skin syndrome — split stratum granulosum), Panton-Valentine leukocidin (PVL, necrotizing pneumonia/skin infections in CA-MRSA). Treatment: MRSA → vancomycin/daptomycin; MSSA → nafcillin/oxacillin.

cards.front: HIV: life cycle steps and antiretroviral drug targets
cards.back: 1. Attachment: gp120 binds CD4 + CCR5/CXCR4 (entry inhibitors: maraviroc/CCR5, enfuvirtide/fusion). 2. Reverse transcription: RNA → DNA (NRTIs/NNRTIs). 3. Integration: viral DNA → host genome (INSTIs: dolutegravir, bictegravir). 4. Translation + processing: protease (PIs: ritonavir, darunavir). 5. Budding.

cards.front: Clostridium difficile: pathogenesis, risk factors, diagnosis, treatment
cards.back: Spore-forming, toxin A (enterotoxin) + toxin B (cytotoxin) → pseudomembranous colitis. Risk: antibiotics (clindamycin, fluoroquinolones, cephalosporins), PPI use, hospitalization, age >65. Diagnosis: stool GDH + toxin A/B EIA or PCR (NAAT). Treatment: mild-moderate: oral vancomycin or fidaxomicin; severe/complicated: oral vanco + IV metronidazole ± colectomy. Recurrent: bezlotoxumab or FMT.

cards.front: Viral hepatitis: serological markers for HBV
cards.back: HBsAg: active infection. Anti-HBs: immunity (vaccination or recovery). HBeAg: high infectivity/active replication. Anti-HBe: seroconversion (↓ viral replication). Anti-HBc IgM: acute infection. Anti-HBc IgG: prior exposure (+ anti-HBs = resolved; alone = window period). HBV DNA: viral load. Treatment: tenofovir, entecavir for chronic HBV.
